sentences.text: In the ghetto was-- it wasn't exactly for-- not for everybody.
It was dangerous or whatever.
But in the ghetto, Germans used to come around into our houses, and they used to kill, and take out whatever they wanted from the houses, from people.
After the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after they evacuated everybody to another camp, they released the ghetto.
They killed about 1,680 people.
They were killed by shelling, by guns, and by-- with rifles.
Whatever they had.
Did I witness the shootings and the killing?
We were taken by group maybe of 20.
We were taken out from the group, which we were first taken to the-- we were staying in the camp for work.
So they took us out.
We should bury the dead people which they killed.
We did undress them.
We had to take out their teeth-- gold teeth.
If anybody had a ring, or anything, we have to cut the fingers off to take out the ring.
And there was a lot of different kind of work.
After we undressed the-- the people, the dead bodies, we had to bring into the excavation and throw them in.
It happens once to a friend of mine.
He was working together with me.
And he brought in the dead bodies to throw into the ditch, a assessment kicked him in the back, and he fell down the embankment to the grave.
But he couldn't get out, because it was very steep.
It was about maybe 10 feet deep.
So I was-- I was right next to him.
And I saw what happened.
[INAUDIBLE] He was going to be dead together with everybody.
So I pulled-- I tried-- I gave him my hand.
I laid it down-- I laid down on-- on my back, and I gave my hand to pull him out from there.
I pulled him out.
And as a matter of fact, he is now alive in New York.
What's his name?
His name is [INAUDIBLE] He was working the same thing with me.
They were kicked me back into the grave.
They didn't make any-- any difference to them, but how you get killed.
If you're taken-- you're taken to Treblinka or killed right there.
What we're talking?
We were all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like we resignated from life.
We didn't care either way.
It was like, you couldn't do anything.
You couldn't run away.
You couldn't--
If you tried to run away, you get killed.
If you don't get killed by the Germans, you get killed by the Polack.
They were selling peoples' lives for a pound of sugar.
Yes.
A friend of mine, he was a-- he was a doctor.
He had a family.
There were six people, I think.
And he arranged with one family, a Polish family, not far away from the city, that they're going to hide in there until the war is over.
But somebody found out that they're hiding in there.
And in the middle of the night, they came and they throw grenades into the bunker where they were.
And after this, they took out the two Polish-- two of the family-- Polish-- and they shot them.
So at the time, there was no place where to run.
There was some Polacks, which they put their life into their hands.
And they tried to hide some Jews.
But 99% didn't.
Either they were afraid, or they just cooperated with the German SS or SR, whatever.
In camp, see I had-- I was-- I was-- I had a girlfriend before the war.
It wasn't any-- any close girlfriend, but when we could get the time, we used to come together.
We used to go out together to some friends and so on.
Her brother used to live in [NON-ENGLISH], Lodz.
Strovchinski.
My brother?
Her brother.
Her brother's name was Strovchinski.
Her name was Strovchinski.
And he used to live in Lodz.
But during the war, he came back home to his parents.
And when the ghetto started up in-- in-- in the city, he was the chief of the ghetto.
He had a lot to do with this-- the president of the ghetto.
And after the-- the-- the-- they-- they closed the ghetto, he became like a supervisor to the-- how they say-- a supervisor to the administration of the small ghetto.
So at the time, when we went out, after the-- after the-- we should-- after they send out everybody, I had my brothers.
I had two brothers, a brother-in-law.
And I had an uncle and some cousins.
They were all in camp.
And I was with-- I was with my girlfriend's brother, together.
I lived with them together.
I didn't live with my brother together.
I lived with him together.
And I wasn't afraid.
I didn't work.
I just went out.
They were [? every ?]-- saw them every day or second or third day.
They were then they called the Jews some place and killed him.
So they send-- they crawled into the camp.
We should take them and bury them.
So we used to go out with the-- [? whatever ?] with the-- with the wagon and take them on the cemetery, and we buried them.
But the rest-- otherwise I wasn't working.
The only-- the only work I did in the camp is I used to work for the Germans.
I used to go out every morning.
They used to-- I [INAUDIBLE] used to come in a car to pick me up.
So I took another guy with me.
I took-- the other guy's name was Monek Laupter.
He passed away a few years back.
He should help at work.
So I worked there for two or three days.
And then I was off for weeks.
Floor laying, floor cleaning, floor scraping.
What they doing here, they now have machines.
They do floor sanding.
But we never sanded floors because we had to scrape them.
They should be smooth and finish them.
So, I worked-- I used to work for the Gestapo.
Took the biggest Jewish building in the city and they made it a headquarters.
So I used to go there to work.
I-- I worked for the Gestapo, for the chief of the Gestapo, and the name was [INAUDIBLE].
He used to come down every day or second day or third day, and he used to throw me packages of cigarettes.
But when something happens and they had to do some killings, he was there, the first.
And normally, a guy like this, he wouldn't have anything to do with a Jew.
I was working in his apartment.
He closed the door.
And he told me to sit together with him by the table and eat lunch.
For this man?
Oh, maybe two months, three months maybe.
I don't remember exactly.
But what they had-- I used to go there.
He used to tell the-- the untersturmfuhrer, the [GERMAN], that they should give me, after work, they should give me some bread.
So they used to come and bring me bread.
They had the bread.
What they used to take away from the-- from the jail.
So they brought the bread, and they distributed to us.
I didn't need at that time, the bread, because I had enough money to buy bread.
But I was working there, and there was other boys working.
So I took my bread.
I gave it to them.
They should have something to eat because they didn't have any-- any help.
We had been in camp-- I was the [? magaziner ?] of all the clothes which was taken after the-- how do you say-- [NON-ENGLISH].
After they evacuated all the-- all the Jews from the city.
So we took all those clothes, whatever was left in the houses.
We took from the houses.
We took it into the camp.
And we made like a magazine.
We-- we storaged everything there.
And then there were left 900 people in one camp, and 900 people in another.
Two factories.
So we used to take this clothes and make packages and distribute every Tuesday.
They used to come in to the Jewish chief of the camp.
And he used to give them a slip.
He should get a package.
And when he got [INAUDIBLE], the slip, he used to come to me.
He had to come to you because there wasn't ever anybody else.
And I used to make a package for the guy for 100,000 zlotys or for 200,000 or for 10,000 zlotys.
In this position, it was two years.
Yeah, I was treated.
The only thing I had to go out to the Gestapo and work there.
And if somebody got killed outside from-- from Jews, I had to go out with another guy and take them to the burial, to the cemetery.
At the end of this three years period-- let me tell you before the end of that time-- while we were in camp there in the factory, we were planning to run out.
It was made a plan complete with everything.
I used to steal guns and grenades from the Gestapo because they never checked me.
In the Gestapo.
When I was working in the Gestapo, I used to steal them there and the magazines.
I had a [GERMAN] which they picked us up every day, every morning to work, and 4:30, they used to take us all back to the camp.
He used to take out his Browning, his gun.
And he said, you see this gun?
With this gun I'm going to shoot you.
I told him, I'm dead already a long time.
You can't do anything to me.
And that was about every couple of days.
He repeated and repeated until it happened he was the [? magaziner ?] of the Gestapo.
And one day the chief of the Gestapo went up to the magazine, he just wanted to take a look.
And before he go to the magazine, there was a watchman, a German from the Gestapo, and he had a list what was stored in the magazine.
So he took from him the list and he goes into the-- to the magazine, and he-- he looks.
He looks what they're there.
Ten bags of sugar.
There's not one left.
There's 10 bags of potatoes that was left too.
So there was a lot of things left.

So when he noticed it, he said right away, where you start to scream murder that they should bring that force, that [? magaziner. ?] They brought him and he asked him, what happened to all those things.
He says, I don't know.
He used to steal and give to his girlfriends.
You know, so-- So the chief of the Gestapo, he says, you stole everything.
So he tells him, I didn't steal that from you, I stole it from the Jews.
He said, if it was here, it was mine and everybody else which lives in this building.
You have no right to steal anything from here.
Half an hour later he tore off his [NON-ENGLISH], you what I mean.
And he called in two Gestapo men, and they took away his gun, everything, and they took him right away to the train.
They send him to Russia, to deep Russia.
That time I got rid of him.
So I wasn't worried about him anymore.
Stealing the guns, I used to bring them to the camp.
I used to hide them and bury them underneath the barrack.
Sure.
As a matter of fact, my brother-in-law and his three brothers and a brother-in-law and one guy from Lodz, they organized.
They bought on somebody around, they bought one or two guns.
And so they run away from the camp.
They wanted to run into the forest to-- They-- they got caught.
Right on the spot there where they got caught, they killed three of them.
Three of my brothers-- two of my brothers-- my brothers with [INAUDIBLE] and one brother-in-law.
And the other three, they brought it to our camp, for my brother-in-law and his brother and one from Lodz.
The next morning, they made an appeal at the camp, and they hung him in camp there.
We had to stay in line and watch while they hung him.
Zajonczkowski.
[INAUDIBLE] Zajonczkowski.
How I felt-- we were-- normally a person would feel something.
But we were so hardened because our thinking wasn't how we felt, but our thinking was that within the next hour or half an hour, we might be dead.
And somebody's going to have to bury us.
So we didn't care what happens.
The only thing we cared about running away, staying alive.
But if we couldn't run away, we knew we were going to be killed.
After seeing what happened to your brother-in-law, did that change your plans about trying to escape?
No.
It didn't change my mind because it was the same thing.
No.
We didn't try, but we planned to escape.
But after they killed, they-- they killed-- and they, the boys, my brother-in-law and the one from Lodz, when they interrogated them, they asked them, why did you run away?
And they said, because they were talking to run away from the camp.
Who was talking?
So they knew a couple of names.
They gave a couple of names.
The guy which was made together, him and another guy.
When they hung him in the camp next day, they took those guys out and they send them to Auschwitz.
They didn't kill them there.
But they send them to Auschwitz.
Where you amongst those people who were named?
The names of those people-- was-- was my brother-in-law was [INAUDIBLE] Zajonczkowski.
This was later.
We were sent to Auschwitz about five months later, four or five months later.
As soon as they-- they-- let me go back to the-- as soon as they took my-- my-- my friend away, the whole group fall apart.
There was nothing anybody could plan because everybody was afraid.
We had-- in-- in-- in-- in the camp, we had, not many, but individuals which used to go out to the Gestapo and tell them what we were doing.
They would squeal on us.
And that's how the Gestapo I found out what and when, but after-- after this incident, we lost everything.
We had connections with Gentile on the outside of the ghetto, the camp.
And the people they were waiting for us to get out.
And from there on, they would connect us to some kind of organization which were at the forests.
But when they took them away, there was no people which could make any plans because everything was too fresh.
And then in three or four months later, they took us all together.
And they sent us to Auschwitz.
